bugs.description: The GET /users/me route's `response_model` was changed from `UserPublic` to `User`. `UserPublic` is the public projection (no secrets); `User` is the table model, which includes the `hashed_password` column. With `response_model=User`, FastAPI now serializes the caller's password hash into every /users/me response. The change is a single identifier swap and the diff shows nothing obviously dangerous, you only see the leak if you know that `User` exposes `hashed_password` while `UserPublic` does not (that distinction lives in models.py, not in this hunk). Sensitive-data exposure that needs cross-file knowledge of the two schemas rather than pattern matching.

The `is_active` enforcement was removed from `get_current_user`. Previously a disabled user (`is_active = False`) was rejected with 400 Inactive user on every authenticated request; now only existence is checked. As a result, deactivating or suspending an account no longer takes effect, any already-issued token for a disabled user keeps working everywhere `CurrentUser` is required. Account deactivation becomes a no-op, an authorization-lifecycle bug that is silent because login still rejects inactive users (only the per-request guard is gone).

injection.replace_with:
user = session.get(User, token_data.sub)
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="User not found")
    return user

bugs.description: `update_user` still computes `extra_data['hashed_password']` from the submitted password but no longer passes it: `db_user.sqlmodel_update(user_data, update=extra_data)` became `db_user.sqlmodel_update(user_data)`. `user_data` carries the plaintext `password` field, which is not a column on `User` (the column is `hashed_password`), so the password change is silently dropped, the user keeps their old password and the freshly computed hash is discarded. An admin password reset (and the password-reset flow that calls this) appears to succeed but does nothing, leaving stale or compromised credentials in place. The dropped `update=extra_data` is easy to miss because `extra_data` is still built right above it.

The `owner_id` foreign key's `ondelete` was changed from `CASCADE` to `SET NULL`, but the column is still declared `nullable=False`. SET NULL tells the database to null the FK of dependent rows when a user is deleted, which immediately violates the NOT NULL constraint, so deleting a user that owns items now raises an integrity error instead of cascade-deleting their items. It also conflicts with the `cascade_delete=True` still set on the `User.items` relationship. A self-contradictory FK definition (`nullable=False` + `ondelete=SET NULL`) that only surfaces on a delete and only if you know how the two settings interact.

`update_item` changed `item_in.model_dump(exclude_unset=True)` to `exclude_unset=False`. With `exclude_unset=False` the dump includes every field of `ItemUpdate` at its default, so a PATCH/PUT that sends only `title` will also carry `description=None` and `item.sqlmodel_update(update_dict)` will overwrite the stored description with null. The result is silent data loss on partial updates: editing one field blanks any field the client did not resend. The change looks innocuous, the two `exclude_unset` values read as equivalent unless you reason about partial-update semantics.

The access-token TTL changed from `timedelta(minutes=settings.ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES)` to `timedelta(days=...)`. The same numeric setting is now interpreted as DAYS, so a value meant to be minutes (the template's constant is 60*24*8 minutes) becomes that many DAYS, the access token is effectively non-expiring. Access tokens are bearer credentials with no server-side revocation, so a stolen or leaked token stays valid for years. A one-word unit change (minutes to days) that reads like an ordinary timedelta and only bites if you connect the unit to what the setting actually holds.
